text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 26 April 2016 

Dr Ayesha Khan 
Consultant Neurologist  
Centre for Epilepsy  
King’s College Hospital, Denmark Hill, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Ayesha Khan, 

Mr Thomas Greene, DOB: 22-09-1976, Hospital No: K9472103 NHS No. 7845129036  
12 Brook Lane, Camberwell, London SE5 0DN  

Diagnosis: 

Focal epilepsy with focal impaired-awareness seizures; improved control on current regimen 

Aetiology: 

Remote right mesial temporal sclerosis on MRI (stable) 

Other Medical Problems: 

Migraine without aura (infrequent), mild asthma well-controlled on PRN salbutamol 

Management Plan: 

- Continue Lamotrigine 150 mg mane and 200 mg nocte, single brand only.  
- Continue Lacosamide 100 mg twice daily.  
- Buccal midazolam 10 mg for seizures clustering beyond 5 minutes or two events within 30 minutes; education reinforced with patient and partner.  
- Arrange serum lamotrigine and LFT/U&E levels at next visit.  
- Seizure diary to be maintained; advise adherence to regular sleep and hydration.  
- DVLA guidance already discussed; patient not driving at present.  
- Follow-up in six months; earlier review if ED presentations recur.  

Multiple recent ED triage records exist; nursing documentation summarised where relevant. Over January–March, he attended ED on three occasions for brief clusters of focal impaired-awareness episodes, each settling without IV therapy. Nursing notes consistently describe preserved respiration, brief behavioural arrest, orofacial automatisms, and post-event confusion resolving within 20–30 minutes. No generalised tonic–clonic activity was recorded. These episodes correlated with missed evening doses on two occasions and acute intercurrent viral illness on one occasion.

Since the last clinic, we increased evening lamotrigine and introduced lacosamide. Seizure burden has been substantially reduced following dose adjustment, with only eight focal impaired-awareness seizures reported so far this year. He and his partner report shorter events and quicker recovery, with no injuries. He remains in employment and has arranged modified shifts to avoid sleep deprivation. He has tolerated the regime with only mild transient dizziness during lacosamide initiation; no rash, mood change, or diplopia.

I have reviewed ED correspondence and triage summaries with him, reinforced adherence strategies, and provided a written rescue plan consistent with nursing protocols used during his attendances. No further investigations are required at this stage beyond therapeutic drug monitoring. Should breakthrough clusters recur despite adherence, we will consider incremental lacosamide titration or adjunctive perampanel.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: only eight focal impaired-awareness seizures reported so far this year
Seizure burden has been substantially reduced following dose adjustment, with only eight focal impaired-awareness seizures reported so far this year

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit seizure count and timeframe. The letter states: “Seizure burden has been substantially reduced… with only eight focal impaired-awareness seizures reported so far this year.” Clinic date is 26 April 2016, so “so far this year” refers to January through April 2016 (approximately 4 months). Therefore, frequency = 8 seizures over 4 months. No other higher-frequency statement supersedes this. Output normalized as '8 per 4 month'.
seizure_frequency_number: only eight focal impaired-awareness seizures reported so far this year
reference: Seizure burden has been substantially reduced following dose adjustment, with only eight focal impaired-awareness seizures reported so far this year
